speaker: interviewer
text: --if any family members on the train to Bizyn. Were you with any of your family members on the train to Blizyn.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, with my brother, my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nojech and Chuma.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you arrived at Blizyn, describe what happened when the doors were opened and what you saw.

speaker: interviewee
text: When the doors opened from the train, there were SS. They took us on the appel field. And there they start what we brought, what we take in. They start to examine things. And later on, we were put in in the barracks, like they used to call this like for horses or whatever it was. And this was our destiny. There we were. And I remember my brother said, my gosh, what are we going to do? I said to him, Nojech, this is it. Forget about what was yesterday. This, you have to go, and that's all. And then it was an appel the first day on the field. And the head of the SS, his name was Nell, and they start to tell us what to do and how to behave. And the next day-- I don't know for what and why-- they took one man what had come with the same transport with us. His name was Masler. And they took him out in the little forest there, and they shot him. That was the beginning.

speaker: interviewer
text: For what reason was he shot?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have no idea whatsoever.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you arrived at Blizyn, was there a registration process of any sort? Did you have to sit down with an SS person? Was your haircut? Were you given a uniform?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no uniform at all. That's what we did wear. Then whatever they want, they took it away from us. And they registered us. Not the-- the Germans-- there were right away Jewish people, what they did, mostly from Radom. They did come in the morning. We did come in the afternoon. And they did a register. It was very, very simple registration. And then whatever we had to go to work. There were-- a lot of people were-- there-- outside work. The steinbruch they used to call. And that was very, very hard.

speaker: interviewer
text: Translate that. What, what would that be, steinbruch?

speaker: interviewee
text: They'd make cement something, like cement. That's what it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: What does that literally translate as, steinbruchen?

speaker: interviewee
text: That call is in German. I don't know how to translate it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What work were you required to do at Blizyn?

speaker: interviewee
text: I worked in the shop, the uniforms, for they send it away for the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: So describe a typical day for you from the morning to the end of the day.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We had to go to work. And later on, like in the morning, we got some s-- coffee. Don't ask. And then we got-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] at home-- when we went home, we got a soup. The soup was more water than soup, but this is the way that was, and we couldn't help it. There were some people. Well, they had money. And outside the camp from Blizyn, through the fence, some Polish people, they threw it over, some bread. You threw some money. And they were on the-- the Ukrainians, they, they, they-- they'd looked after this. And probably, they give some money to the Ukraines, and they didn't do anything so. Everybody tried to help himself, whatever was possible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you manage to maintain a level of health during your time in Blizyn? Or did you begin to get sick?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah I got sick once. I got a typhus.

speaker: interviewer
text: At Blizyn?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Blizyn. And somehow I survive. A lot of people, my friends, they didn't survive from typhus-- a lot of people.

speaker: interviewer
text: How is it that you recuperated? Did you continue working while you had typhus?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. There was-- it's called a hospital, and I was there, in the hospital. And to be honest, I didn't know even-- I knew typhus, but I know the only thing-- I, I was sick, very sick. And somehow, someway, I survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe the dispensing of food at Blizyn. How would it take place? Did you line up to receive your--

speaker: interviewee
text: Portion.

speaker: interviewer
text: --your, your portion, and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We lined up to receive the por-- like soup. Bread-- sometime they give like 10 people for a bread. And everything, you know, whoever dis-- displayed the bread-- that was just terrible. It was measured so by, by, by in-- by inch. Inch is nothing. That everybody got the same potion. God forbid somebody had a little bit bigger or whatever it is was murder.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe what would happen if somebody felt that somebody else got a bigger portion.

speaker: interviewee
text: Did-- everybody did try not to be like this. They had-- some of them, they made a little scale, you know, from a plate and weighed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there guys that you regularly would share your bread with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mostly. Mostly.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who amongst that group of guys was responsible for weighing the bread or cutting the portions?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was not a responsibility. Whoever ta-- we thought he can do it, he did it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever do it?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, never. Sometime I think-- if I remember correctly-- if we got the ready portion right from the kitchen, too. I don't remember this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any contact between male and female prisoners at Blizyn?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Blizyn, yes. In Blizyn, yes, because it was not divided. So there were some couples there. They were-- they were married, husband and wife, but they, they were not together. They were in a separate barracks. Like I did see often every day my sister. I could have come over there. As a matter of fact, we played soccer in Blizyn. I remember all day, La-- Radom, Piatkow, Starachowice, everybody did play against our team, Tomaszow. And we won. We obvious won. We were the best. So the Jewish-- you know, the Jewish-- what-- he was the head in Blizyn. He did play soccer too. His name was Minzberg. He was a very good guy, very good guy. And we won, we got a extra soup. And you know, the Germans, they did watch us play.

speaker: interviewer
text: Until when were you in Blizyn?

speaker: interviewee
text: Till we when out to this, to Birkenau.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the approximate date?

speaker: interviewee
text: I just-- I just marked it like today. I don't--

speaker: interviewer
text: What year would it have been? 1943? '44?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1944. 1944. Yeah. The only thing I know, we were there till June 1944. And then we went to Oranienburg Sachsenhausen for a short period of time.

speaker: interviewer
text: I just want to stop you. I don't want to get ahead. How were you transported? From Blizyn to Birkenau, how were you transported?

speaker: interviewee
text: Also in the-- in the-- no, in the train.

speaker: interviewer
text: Cattle.

speaker: interviewee
text: The cattle train, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the conditions any different from the conditions--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not different at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: --when you came. How long was the trip?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] I think it was about seven, eight hours, or more. I don't remember correctly. Or more, even.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you arrive at Birkenau in the day or in the night?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the night.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you see when the doors were opened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, did we have it that. That welcome, I will never forget in my life. The yelling, the screaming, the, the, the, the-- what the SS did with the dogs that I, I really don't know how everybody come out alive. Not-- I wouldn't say everybody. But the people from Blizyn, when we did come to Birkenau, I don't know or we had our good name from the Germans from Blizyn that we didn't-- we didn't go in a selection. We went right-- in Birkenau, we went into the shower there, but we didn't go in a selection.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sort of back up a little bit. You said you'll never forget that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Welcome

speaker: interviewer
text: --coming into Birkenau and the yelling and the screaming. Who was yelling? And--

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were they yelling?

speaker: interviewee
text: Raus! Raus! Raus! They made it such that everybody got so scared. Raus, that means, faster, faster, get out, get out.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you got off the train--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah?

speaker: interviewer
text: --did you have to assemble in any certain way, in certain any kind of rows? Or did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: We assembled and march towards the shower. And we didn't know the shower was there in that time right near the crematorium. We didn't know anything about. Maybe some people knew, but I personally didn't know anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were not in a concentration camp uniform at this time. You were still in your regular clothing? Your civilian clothing?

speaker: interviewee
text: In that time in Birkenau, we get the uniforms--

speaker: interviewer
text: When you came in?

speaker: interviewee
text: --the, the, the stripe uniforms. Everybody got.

speaker: interviewer
text: What else did you see as you were coming into Birkenau, before you were even registered? Did you see the other prisoners? Did you see by the crematorium or the chimneys?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't-- didn't know anything about crematoriums or chimneys where-- we went to the, like I said, to the shower. Where there is shower, we saw the chimney. And if we did ask somebody what is this, over there what it is-- for they'd been before-- they said the-- some, they made a joke. They said, oh, oh, you will go with the-- with the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] through the chimney, something like this. The only thing we saw that was was in night, that was the sky-- red, burn all day and night.

speaker: interviewer
text: So describe the registration at Birkenau.

speaker: interviewee
text: The registration was this. The next day, they put the numbers on the hand. And we did-- I did stay with my brother in the same-- next to each other. And we put in the arm like this. And somebody behind, they put the number on. Before people did come, they just put it down here. I have a number right here.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what number is your number?

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you hold your arm up so that we can take a, a shot of it? If you just--

speaker: interviewee
text: You see--

speaker: interviewer
text: If you just hold it--

speaker: interviewee
text: If you stay like this, comes out even. And if you stay like this, comes out the opposite way.

speaker: interviewer
text: If you could just hold your arm like that. And what number is it?

speaker: interviewer
text: And is that actually your number?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because that's a very early number.

speaker: interviewee
text: The only thing they forgot here to put it then, a B.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how do you know they forgot to put a B?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I didn't know. I saw I haven't got a B. My brother's got a B.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his number is what?

speaker: interviewer
text: And the other people that were in that line all had a B before their number?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was overlooked in your case?

speaker: interviewee
text: It looks like it was overlooked. I didn't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What else did the registration consist of? Was your hair cut?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They made it like-- they called us [GERMAN]. You know what that means?

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me.

speaker: interviewee
text: That means the lous-- louse on the strip because it was cut right in the middle, like this. By, by and inch, an inch and a half, a stripe.

speaker: interviewer
text: On your uniform, were there any ident-- identifying designs or colors?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. There were a lot of colors. Some, they had red. Some, they had black. And I forgot what we had. I think J, Jude. Yeah, a yellow-- ma-- Jude. That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a J or a star of David or a triangle?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think was a, a tri-- a triangle there. I don't remember this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you work in Birkenau?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I didn't work at all. I don't know. The only thing there was the head from the barrack, his name was Pinkus. He was from Paris-- a Jewish guy, a very bad guy. For somehow, some reason, he picked me, and he said, you're going to cook for me. And I remember he said, make me some carrots. Cook me some carrots.

speaker: interviewee
text: I put in carrots and put in water. And he came over to me. He said, you said you are a cook. Is this the way to cook carrots? And I looked at him, and he did see that I'm green. And he said, I'm going to knock your teeth out right away. Is this the way that you cook carrot? I said, how? Carrots, you cook on sugar. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So I said, OK. Give me sugar. He said, that's fair. I took yours. I cooked it. I have to give you sugar. I said, from where can I get it? So he sent me into the kitchen because they, they had a little bit more. They could get whatever they want. And go into Sasha and say that I sent you. And you got me sugar. And then, then I cooked the carrots. Where did you cook them, in the barrack?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the barrack.

speaker: interviewer
text: On what did you cook them?

speaker: interviewee
text: They had a little oven and coals and wood. I remember, I cooked the carrots, and then they were ready. I threw it ov-- over the wire, the electric wire, to my sister. And she couldn't-- till now, she doesn't know how I did it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you leave any for--

speaker: interviewee
text: For him?

speaker: interviewer
text: --for him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. The mostly, a little bit for her.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his role was what? He was the blockalteste?

speaker: interviewee
text: Correct. I think he had a trial after the war. They catched him.

speaker: interviewer
text: But he was Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Pinkus was his name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe life in the barracks there at Birkenau.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, in the barracks, you know, some people were sick. And you had to stay on appell, and they couldn't stay. They fall, and they-- oye, oye, oye. It was terrible, just terrible, in one word.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say you had to stand on appell, what would be the translation for that word appell?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were like-- they, they're counting, counting the, the people or nobody did escape or whatever it is. They counted.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe the distribution of food to the prisoners and to you in Birkenau.

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- well, the same thing as it was in Blizyn. They just give it soup. They broughted soup over to every barrack. And people from the barrack, they divide it to everybody. Soup-- I want to had a little bit better, a little bit, you know, [INAUDIBLE] soup, a little bit [INAUDIBLE] soup.

speaker: interviewer
text: At what p-- did you become aware that people were being gassed at Birkenau when you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Later on.

speaker: interviewer
text: While you were still at Birkenau?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you become aware of that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because later on-- right the second day, you knew when we arrived that this is crematoriums, and people are getting gassed there. And like when we were there already, people later on did come from Lodz. And some people, they right away went into the gas and the children. So they children, a lot of children. They came from Wilno. I remember a lot of-- a lot of children.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was it that you were in a position to see them?

speaker: interviewee
text: To see them?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, you could see them from one barrack to the other. You can see them. You could walk there too. Because it was lengthwise, and the barracks was-- they were standing like this, like this, like this. And you could go there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were ever close to the gas chambers, or did you know where they were physically in the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah. Oh yeah. Sure, that was-- it was open.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you mean it was open?

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean that everybody could see it from far away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever see people that were waiting there to--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --go in?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. The only thing, I, I know like, like we went into the shower, yeah? That was a real shower. And they-- what I've been told in the-- in the crematoriums-- maybe it was even the same what I went in, the shower, that they threw it in the gas through the roof. This, I don't know. But we were people who did arrive from Blizyn. They never got gassed, none of us. Maybe later on, somebody got sick, and they took us to the gas chamber.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you ever subject to a selection in Birkenau?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in Birkenau? What length of time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Till November.

speaker: interviewee
text: '44. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That was in Birkenau.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then where?

speaker: interviewee
text: And from Birkenau, we went for a very short period of time to Oranienburg and Sachsenhausen, maybe two weeks altogether.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you transported there?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Sachsenhausen?

speaker: interviewer
text: Well from Birkenau, where did you go first?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Oranienburg.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: We were there I think a few days, a week. And in Sachsenhausen also so the same time. And from Sachsenhausen, we were transport already to Ohrdruf in very good train, in a train, a passenger train.

speaker: interviewer
text: From Birkenau to Oranienburg, how were you transported?

speaker: interviewee
text: By, by also the cattle, the cattle one.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in Oranienburg, you said you were only approximately one week.

speaker: interviewee
text: There wasn't-- we did walk from Oranienburg to Sachsenhausen. It wasn't very far.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you see when you arrived at Oranienburg from Birkenau?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. We did sleep in a-- in a very, very big hole where they made the airplanes. That's all. That's what I did see. We didn't see any other people too. in one camp, we saw one Pollack there, I remember. They were a little bit farther outside. And the-- and same Oranienburg, a little bit farther. We didn't see it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And from Oranienburg, you were transported to Sachsenhausen.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. By foot. Walked.

speaker: interviewer
text: On foot. How long of a walk was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not long. I would say about three hours. We pressed by a little city. We did see German people, and they did see us walking on the street.

speaker: interviewer
text: During that march, was there any assistance given to you by the Germans that you saw walking?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who was guarding you during the march?

speaker: interviewee
text: The same thing, Ukrainians, some Germans too in that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were some people not able to complete the march between--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not as far as I know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know why it was that you were in those locations for such short periods, in Oranienburg and Sachsenhausen?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have no idea. I have no idea.

speaker: interviewer
text: Approximately how many of you were marching from Oranienburg to Sachsenhausen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, would be about-- for sure about 1,000 or between 1,000 and 1,500, for sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many guards would you say there were? It It didn't have to be too many guards either because everybody was afraid anyway. I don't know. I really don't know. Were you s-- were you together with any family members still?

speaker: interviewee
text: With mine brother all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your brother Nojech?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. All the time, and some friend too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you and him ever have any discussions about trying to escape or about whether you were going to live through this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not me. He had some discussion I know with some other people. But I said to him, what are you going to do? He, he wouldn't be able to do it, not him.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to stop there and continue on the next tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
